sanskrit_devanagari:
पूर्वाभ्यासेन तेनैव ह्रियते ह्यवशोऽपि सः ।
जिज्ञासुरपि योगस्य शब्दब्रह्मातिवर्तते ॥ ४४ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
pūrvābhyāsena tenaiva hriyate hy avaśo 'pi saḥ |
jijñāsur api yogasya śabda-brahmātivartate || 44 ||

anvaya.meaning: the inquirer / one who wishes to know

translations.verbatim_quote: By virtue of the divine consciousness of his previous life, he automatically becomes attracted to the yogic principles – even without seeking them. Such an inquisitive transcendentalist stands always above the ritualistic principles of the scriptures.

By virtue of the divine consciousness of his previous life, he automatically becomes attracted to the yogic principles—even without seeking them. Such an inquisitive transcendentalist stands always above the ritualistic principles of the scriptures.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1581 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Advanced yogīs are not very much attracted to the rituals of the scriptures, but they automatically become attracted to the yoga principles, which can elevate them to complete Kṛṣṇa consciousness, the highest yoga perfection.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.44 Hi, for; tena eva, by that very; purva-abhyasena, past practice-the powerful habit formed in the past life; hiryate, he, the yogi who had fallen from Yoga, is carried forward; avasah api, even inspite of himself. If he had not committed any act which could be characterized as unrigthe
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-44-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara emphasizes the *powerful habit* formed in the past life: by that very past practice (pūrva-abhyāsa) the yogi who had fallen from Yoga is carried forward, even in spite of himself (avaśaḥ api). The latent saṁskāra acts as a current strong enough to draw the practitioner back into the practice without conscious volition. The mere wish-to-know (jijñāsu) of yoga already places one beyond the ritual-performer (śabda-brahma).

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.43 – 6.44 There, in that existence, he regains the mental disposition for Yoga that he had in the previous birth. Like one awakened from sleep, he strives again from where he had left before attaining complete success. He strives so as not to be defeated by impediments. This person who h
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-44-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja preserves 6.43-44 as a single unit: the prior practice-substrate carries the practitioner forward like one waking from sleep. Even an aspirant who only desires to know yoga (jijñāsu) is already lifted past the ritual-Vedic plane (śabda-brahma) — the practice operates above the level of mere prescribed performance.

positions.rendering: the ritualistic principles of the scriptures
the level of mere ritual-performer (Vedic prescriptions)
the ritual-Vedic plane (the level the karma-kāṇḍa addresses)

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Śabda-brahma literally means 'brahman-of-sound' — the Veda as ritual-prescription. All three traditions read the verse as claiming that even an aspirant who merely *wishes to know* yoga has already transcended the level the karma-kāṇḍa addresses. Prabhupada flattens to 'ritualistic principles'; Shankara reads it as the prescribed-performance level; Ramanuja reads it scoped to the karma-kāṇḍa specifically. The structural claim is uncontroversial across traditions: the substrate of prior practice operates above mere ritual.

The compound avaśaḥ-api is striking: 'even unwilling / without will / helpless.' The prior practice is strong enough that the practitioner is drawn back into it without conscious volition. Shankara sharpens this to 'in spite of himself.' Prabhupada softens to 'automatically.' Ramanuja gives the simile of awakening. All three preserve the non-volitional pull of the saṁskāra.

literal_meaning: By that very prior practice he is carried, even unwilling. Even one who merely wishes to know yoga goes beyond the brahman-of-sound (the ritualistic Vedic prescription).
traditional_meaning_consensus: 6.44 is the second half of the consolation Krishna gives Arjuna across 6.40-45. The prior practice (pūrva-abhyāsa) is strong enough to carry the practitioner forward without volition (avaśaḥ api hriyate) — Shankara: 'in spite of himself'; Ramanuja: 'like one awakened from sleep'; Prabhupada: 'automatically, even without seeking them.' Furthermore, even an aspirant who has merely begun to inquire into yoga (jijñāsu) is already established above the level of ritual-Vedic prescription (śabda-brahma ativartate). The traditional consensus reads the verse as a structural claim about the cumulative force of prior practice and the elevation conferred by even early yogic inquiry.
